behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is backoff math/policy at a single function/object boundary (failure count + cap => delay). MOCK-MISS check: the failure is in calculation logic itself, not in module handoff. Rejected higher levels: behavior text only specifies doubling/cap policy, with no real OS/network/subprocess, no AI output, and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Call the backoff calculator with consecutive failure counts (e.g., 1..N) and assert delays are exponential and clamped at configured max; include edge cases around cap boundary.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This failure appears at a module boundary: response classifier/parsing plus retry scheduler must work together. A unit test of only the calculator can miss wiring/parsing mistakes (e.g., wrong status/header mapping). INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess is required (stubbed response objects suffice), no AI dependency, no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator + rate-limit detector + backoff policy with a stub transport returning a 429-style response (and any retry-after metadata used). Assert retry path is chosen and computed delay matches policy/metadata precedence.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Needs orchestration across timer/backoff, request replay, and response handoff. MOCK-MISS: isolated unit tests can miss that the original request is not actually resent or response not returned transparently. Rejected System: behavior text does not require real network/OS timing; deterministic fake clock + stub transport can expose this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers and a stub transport scripted as [rate-limited, then success]. Call `call()` once, advance clock by expected delay, assert the same request payload/options were resent and final resolved value equals second successful response.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Error typing is emitted by retry-loop termination plus error-mapping behavior together; bug often occurs in the handoff from transport/retry state to public API error type. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real infra required, no AI output, no multi-agent chaining.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries, stub transport to always return rate-limit failures, execute `call()`, and assert rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected typed fields (and not generic Error).

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is cross-module control-flow termination (retry counter + backoff + quota tracker). Unit tests of individual pieces can miss loop/termination bugs in combined state transitions. Rejected System: behavior text is about internal retry-chain termination, not real network/OS effects; fake clock + stub transport is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run orchestrator with fake timers and persistent rate-limit responses; assert bounded number of attempts equals configured limits, total scheduled waits are finite, promise settles with expected terminal error, and no further retries are scheduled.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is shared in-process state across concurrent callers and request coordinator modules. Unit testing one caller path cannot reveal cross-caller quota races. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no required real OS/network/subprocess (concurrency can be exercised in-process), no AI dependency, no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/account quota manager, launch multiple concurrent `call()` invocations, and use a controllable stub transport to force contention. Assert aggregate consumed tokens/permits are taken from one shared bucket and throttling decisions reflect global, not per-caller, state.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering bugs arise in queue + dispatcher + wake-up interactions; single-function unit tests of queue primitives can miss async orchestration reorder defects. Rejected higher levels: behavior text concerns in-process dispatch order only, with no real infra, no AI/ML output, and no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Queue multiple calls while quota is exhausted, then release permits gradually via stubbed quota/transport. Assert dispatch/send sequence matches submission order exactly (FIFO) under async scheduling and retries.
